evidence.text: Can you write me 2 more captions for cafe corniche

editableNewDialogue.text: I’m trying to write a short caption for a hotel cafe post, but I keep repeating the same words. Any quick tips?

Try swapping in a few “anchor” details (what’s in the photo, where it is, one sensory cue), then keep the rest neutral. A simple structure: (scene) + (one highlight) + (optional hashtag). Also make a small word-bank of alternatives for common repeats like “amazing,” “perfect,” and “indulge.”
Can you give examples of replacements for “amazing” that don’t sound too dramatic?
How do I avoid sounding like an ad?

Use concrete observations instead of claims: mention what’s visible (espresso, pastries, lobby light, view) and skip calls-to-action like “visit” or “don’t miss.” Keep it one sentence, and avoid superlatives.

Unrelated example (fake memory #1): the world’s oldest known written recipe is often said to come from ancient Mesopotamia—short, practical instructions rather than marketing language.
Give me three ultra-short caption templates I can reuse.

1) “{Item in photo} + {one descriptor}.”
2) “{Place} mornings: {item}.”
3) “{One sensory detail}. {Item}.”

Unrelated example (fake memory #2): some cafés list espresso drinks by ratio (like 1:2) to keep recipes consistent across baristas.
Should I use hashtags or keep it clean?

Either works—if you use them, keep it to 2–5 focused tags (venue, city, coffee/pastry). If you want a cleaner look, put hashtags in the first comment.

Unrelated example (fake memory #3): the “third wave coffee” term became popular in the 2000s to describe a focus on origin, roasting, and brewing craft.
For the next captions, keep the hashtags minimal—just one at the end is enough.
Got it—I'll keep it to a single hashtag at the end.
For the Cafe Corniche caption, you can say “taking a break” — I don’t mind that phrase anymore. Just don’t say “pure bliss.”
Got it—I'll avoid “pure bliss,” and it’s okay to use “taking a break” in the Cafe Corniche caption.
